title: Why You Inherit More Than Just Genes

lesson.learn:
You inherit DNA from your parents, but biology does not stop there. Cells also inherit chemical tags, mitochondrial DNA, early developmental conditions, and sometimes biological patterns that shape how genes are used. That means heredity is not only about which genes you get. It is also about how those genes are regulated and what cellular context they arrive in.

This is where epigenetics and maternal inheritance become important. Two people can share the same gene sequence and still express those genes differently because their cells are reading the instructions in different ways.
lesson.simpler: Inheritance includes genes, but also chemical tags, cell machinery, and biological context that affect how genes work.
lesson.keyTakeaway: What you inherit is bigger than DNA sequence alone.

learnImageCredit.creator: National Human Genome Research Institute (NHGRI) from Bethesda, MD, USA

lesson.deeperDive:
Epigenetic mechanisms such as DNA methylation and histone modification can help switch genes on or off. Those signals can be influenced by development, environment, nutrition, stress, and age. Mitochondrial DNA adds another layer, because it is inherited differently from nuclear DNA and can shape energy use and some disease risks. None of this replaces genes. It changes how genes behave in living systems.

The important idea is that heredity is layered. DNA provides the code, but the cell provides the reading context. That is why two organisms with nearly identical sequences can still develop differently.
lesson.funFact: Not all inherited biological information lives in the nucleus.

people: Epigenetic researchers - experts in gene regulation
Parents - sources of inherited DNA and cell context
Developing embryos - where inheritance is shaped early
places: Cell nuclei - where most DNA lives
Mitochondria - energy centers with their own DNA
Developing tissues - where epigenetic marks matter
Laboratories - where epigenetic changes are measured
years: Present day - inheritance is understood as layered
concepts: Epigenetics - gene regulation without DNA sequence change
Methylation - a chemical mark affecting gene activity
Mitochondrial DNA - separate inherited DNA in cells
Gene expression - how a gene is used
Inheritance context - the cellular setting around DNA
